raw_result.description: Telegram provides messaging APIs for bots and full Telegram clients, plus a Gateway service for delivering login codes through Telegram.
raw_result.auth_notes: Official Telegram Bot API docs require a bot token from @BotFather in the request path. Official Telegram API docs for full clients require registering an application to obtain api_id and api_hash; this is a developer credential scheme not cleanly mapped to OAuth/API key/Basic, so recorded as other.
raw_result.gating_reason: Official docs describe creating bots via @BotFather and obtaining api_id/api_hash for your own application via my.telegram.org, with Bot API access available at no cost for standard usage.

api_surface.breadth: Telegram exposes a broad HTTP Bot API for messaging, media, payments, web apps, and admin flows; official docs also describe the fuller Telegram API for client applications, but the gathered evidence most directly supports the HTTP Bot API surface.
api_surface.mcp_notes: No official Telegram MCP server was evidenced in the gathered official Telegram documentation.

raw_result.rate_limit_notes: Official Bot FAQ evidence gathered indicated bot limits such as about 1 message per second in private chats, 20 messages per minute in groups, and roughly 30 messages per second for bulk notifications, with paid broadcasts available for higher throughput.
raw_result.notable_signals: Telegram maintains multiple official developer surfaces: Bot API, the fuller Telegram API for client apps, and Telegram Gateway for authentication codes; Bot API docs explicitly position the interface as HTTP-based and free for developers for standard usage.
raw_result.human_review_reason: A structured field remains uncertain: api_surface.type. The evidence clearly supports an HTTP/REST Bot API, but the gathered pages also reference the fuller Telegram API for client apps without enough direct evidence in this pass to classify the overall app-level surface as strictly REST versus Both.
